speaker: interviewer
text: Chuck, we were talking about you coming to Detroit. You stayed at a hotel. You came to Detroit. You'd already learned how many languages at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I spoke French, Yiddish, and I knew a little bit of Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you able to communicate with your cousin once you got off the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, no problem. She spoke Yiddish. So it wasn't any problem communicating with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to your aunt's home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, in Detroit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you spoke Yiddish at your aunt's home.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to guess at this point. And then you had to go to school in the United States and learn another language.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had what they called Americanization classes where you had different students from different countries. And I went to Hutchins Intermediate. And we spent the whole day in the one-room class with one teacher who taught us English. I think I learned more English grammar than I did in the four years of high school afterwards in that six-month period, which was a good experience. But I remember going in the street, on the playground, when I first came over, and kids were yelling. They were playing baseball. I remember that. And they were yelling something and I felt really stupid because I didn't know what they were talking. And I remember they were making fun because I didn't understand-- didn't understand what they were saying, which was frustrating at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you eventually make friends with kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Yeah. Once I began to speak a little bit, I could communicate with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where'd you live in Detroit? What streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: On Linwood Avenue between Puritan and Fenkell. And I went to Hallie school after the Americanization class.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Americanization class.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were, what, about 12 when you came over here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I went to-- when I came here, I went to Hebrew school and learned how to read Hebrew. And I had my bar mitzvah at 12 because if you're an orphan, you have it a year earlier. So I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you have it at? Do you remember what synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was a synagogue Shaarey Shomayim. In fact, the rabbi's still in this area. Rabbi Goldman was the rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come across your brother and your sister again? How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well let's see. Before I came to the United States, my brother was going to Israel. On his way to Israel, he stopped in France. I guess he knew where I was staying and I saw him then. And then I didn't see him till he came to visit me, about 10 years ago. So after all these years he came with his wife. And they stayed here for two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your reunion with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very emotional. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came about four years ago, I think it was. She and her son came. And she stayed here for a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you keep in touch with them--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Periodically. About every other month we-- we talk to each other on the phone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you find that there's any language barrier, or are you still--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a hard time communicating in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they speak Hebrew and--

speaker: interviewee
text: They speak Hebrew and Polish. And my-- my Yiddish isn't too good, so-- and I don't speak Hebrew. So-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You had your bar mitzvah, stayed with your aunt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have an uncle too, or just an aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The uncle passed away when I came to this country. So it was my aunt and her two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your aunt's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Anna Stoffer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The children, it was David Stoffer and Bess. She was married at the time, Bess Levitt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you started high school. How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started high school back in 1950, I believe it was. Mumford High School. Third graduating class from Mumford High School.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a brand new school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brand new school, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a lot of friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had quite a few friends there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames, anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they used to call-- because I spoke French, they called me Frenchy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about girlfriends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a few. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: You would have graduated in what year then?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go into the military? Did you go to school? Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. Went to a community college first and then I went to Wayne State and graduated in '59 as a teacher of French and Spanish. And I started-- oh, no, wait a minute. Didn't start teaching full time. I went into the Army in '59, April of '59. And then when I came out in October, I started teaching full time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you begin your first job at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wayne Junior High School. And then I went to another junior high school, Mettetal. And then I went to Henry Ford High School in Detroit. And I taught there till 1966, July of this year. And I'm retired now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're retired. Did you find any incidents of antisemitism when you were growing up in the Detroit area? Did you have anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I was-- I was really surprised. There really wasn't-- that I remember-- any negative things that happened as far as antisemitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very different from--

speaker: interviewee
text: From what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: --where you had come from.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. Yeah, you weren't afraid to say that you were Jewish and that you spoke Yiddish or whatever. It was quite different.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet Mrs. Mudryk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's-- [LAUGHTER] That's a story in itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we've got time.

speaker: interviewee
text: A friend of mine gave me the number. And I had just broken up with this young lady I was engaged to. And after our break up this friend gave me her number. I called her up and we went out on a blind date. And from there it was history. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: It'll be 37 years in December.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have children as well, don't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Two sons and a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have a question to you about your brother and sister again, in a way. They've come here to see you. Have you been to Israel to see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That's-- that's the other thing we-- I hope one of these days that we'll go and see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever talk about your experiences in Europe when you came over here right away? Or did you tell people what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I think it was more-- I was blocking it out more than anything else. They didn't ask me exactly, you know, how I felt or what I went through. And I wouldn't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about now? What made you want to talk about it now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't want to have people forget in future generations of what had really happened and that history can repeat itself unless we learn for what had happened. And as a teacher, I remember showing a French movie to my students, And when I told them what the story is about, they had no idea what I was talking about. So I had to give them a little history background on World War II and Hitler and the invasion and all that. They had no idea what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if our young people don't know what happened 50 years ago, I can imagine what it's going to be like another 50 years unless we tell them and remind them that it did happen. And I'm an eyewitness. Do you think your experiences-- or do you feel that your experiences during the war affected you religiously in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think it's given me more faith-- faith in God because you often ask yourself, why-- why was I saved and why weren't some of the other people-- my parents and my cousin. There must be a reason why-- why I'm here. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to add to tell us that we might have forgotten? We seem to have gone almost a little too rapidly in some areas. And I'd really like if you think that I've forgotten to ask you anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember one scene when we went to a village. And either the Germans or the Ukrainians murdered people and they were laying in the street with stomach open and bullets in their heads. And that stands out, how horrible that was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were only six years old at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you can remember that very vividly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's been over 50 years since the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any special message that you'd like to tell the world other than, you know-- I know you feel it's very important that we not forget. You've made that very clear, and I'm glad to hear it. But is there any other message that you'd like to tell your children or the world or anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: That a holocaust like this can happen again unless we're more aware of some of the things that went on in the past. And let's not have it again. And people should take it more seriously and not-- not say, well, it doesn't affect me, so I don't care. Well, it may affect you eventually if you let it go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that doesn't necessarily mean that it can be a Jewish holocaust either, does it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: That can be for anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look what happened in Bosnia and all these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Chuck, at this point I believe we're finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Unless you can think of anything else that I've forgotten.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I think that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I'd like to thank you on behalf of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're very indebted to you for giving us your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chuck, can you tell me about this photograph? Where it was taken? What city?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I think it was taken in Dabrowica. And this my mother's family. The gentlemen with the beard is my great-grandfather, I think. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't remember the name of the great-grandfather. The grandfather's name was David. This is his father. And the other people are the children and the grandchildren and some friends and the husbands and wives.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name would be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Becheck. Becheck is the last name of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And we don't-- do you know-- you don't know when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. Have no idea when this was taken. Was before the war, that's all I know. But I don't know when exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't know if your mother is in this picture or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't-- I can't even recognize-- we don't even have any pictures of my mother. All I remember is she was prematurely gray. And I don't even remember what she looked like.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my great-grandfather Becheck. And I don't know when this was taken either.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city? Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would assume it's in the same city, Dabrowica.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come about this photograph, and the other one as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my brother came to visit me about 10 years ago, he had told me that the people in our area-- the survivors, that is-- made a book, included all the pictures-- pictures that they were able to save. And they made a book. And that's how it came to be. And then he sent me a copy of the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who this is a photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a photograph of my grandfather, my mother's father. His name is David Becheck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any idea when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was evidently before the war, but I don't know exactly when.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture, where did this one come from? Also from this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Also from Dabrowica.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. To the right-- the bottom right is my father. Name was Avraham Mudryk. And in the center, gentleman with the beard, is my grandfather, Reuven, and my grandmother next to him. And the gentleman in the back there must be my uncle and his two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your uncle's name, do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't recall the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. Can you tell me where this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would assume it was also Dabrowica.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Date is prewar. That's all I can decipher from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me. High school graduation, 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: From?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mumford High School.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Detroit, Michigan.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. [INAUDIBLE] Can you tell me who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a picture of my family. In the front, young lady on the right is my daughter, Cindy. Next to her in the green blouse is my sister, Sarah, from Israel. She came to visit me. And the next young man is my son, Andy. And next to him is my wife, Sheila. And next to Sheila is my nephew, Avram. That's Sarah's son, from Israel. And in the back I'm standing there behind my daughter. And the middle is my son, Alan, and his wife, Linda, next to him, in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me where this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken at our home in Southfield.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was about four years ago when my sister and her son came to visit us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would be 1992?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. Yeah, around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a picture of my family. And going from right to left, my cousin David Stofer, my cousin Herald Levitt, who is deceased, my cousin Ida, who is also deceased now. And the gentleman with the gray hair is my brother from Israel. And my cousin next to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name is David. My cousin Bess.

speaker: interviewer
text: David--

speaker: interviewee
text: David Mudryk. My cousin Bess Levitt. And next to her is my daughter, Cindy, and my son, Andrew, and my wife, Sheila, on the end. And in the back, way in the back, the lady with the glasses, the sister in law, Cynthia, from Israel. Cynthia Mudryk. And my son Alan next to her. And I'm next to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Charles Mudryk.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. The year this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- taken about 1984, '85, somewhere around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city? Southfield, Michigan. All right. Charles--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, good, because I umed anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why am I not seeing anything? Got to turn the camera on. Oh, I turned the [INAUDIBLE]. We were on. We're going to have to play a game with the wires. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Charles, or, Chuck, I would like you to introduce the people that you're sitting with.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my wife, Sheila, and my son, Andy. And I'm Chuck Mudryk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have two other children who aren't present.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two other children. My oldest son, Alan Mudryk, wasn't able to make it today. And my daughter, Cindy, wasn't able to make it either.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, I'd like to know-- or we'd like to ask you if you have a special message that you would like to give your family in light of your life, being a survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [LAUGHS] there must be a reason why I survived. [PAUSES FOR 29 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I could have a-- a family like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] And I'm very proud of my son Andy. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd also like to ask Sheila and Andy if they have any special message that they would like to give to you. Sheila, if you would  like to say something to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank God he survived, because without-- if he wouldn't have, I wouldn't have had him or met him, or we wouldn't have the family we have. And he wouldn't have influenced as many people as he has.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, Andy, any message to give to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think your experiences are unfortunate, but they've made you very strong and courageous. And I'm very proud of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point I'd like to thank all of you. Chuck, thank you so much, once again. On behalf of the Shoah Visual history Foundation, we thank you very much. You have a lovely family and we wish all of you well in life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: That concludes our interview.
